result.markdown:
--- title: "How to Publish Your Post" subtitle: "A step-by-step guide for PS50 students — from zero to published" toc: true toc-depth: 3 --- This page is everything you need. Read it once end-to-end before you start. It covers: 1. Setting up GitHub (once only) 2. Choosing your stream 3. Writing your post 4. Submitting for publication --- ## Before you write anything: set up GitHub GitHub is where all the posts live. Think of it like a shared Google Drive, except it tracks every change anyone ever makes, and publishing a new post is as simple as sending a pull request (explained below). You only do this setup once. ### Step 1 — Create a GitHub account Go to [github.com](https://github.com) and sign up with your Goldsmiths email. Your username will be visible on your post, so pick something sensible. ### Step 2 — Fork the blog repository ::: {.callout-note} ## What is a fork? A fork is your own personal copy of the blog. You make changes in your copy, then ask for them to be merged into the real site. This means you can't accidentally break anything for everyone else. ::: 1. Go to the blog repository at `https://github.com/YOURNAME/ps50-blog` 2. Click the **Fork** button (top right) 3. Keep all the defaults and click **Create fork** You now have your own copy at `https://github.com/YOURUSERNAME/ps50-blog`. ### Step 3 — Install the tools you need You need two things installed on your computer: **Quarto** — the publishing tool that turns your `.qmd` file into a webpage. Download from: [quarto.org/docs/get-started](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) **R + RStudio** — for running code in your posts (optional if you're not including data analysis). Download R from: [r-project.org](https://www.r-project.org) Download RStudio from: [posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/) **GitHub Desktop** — a simple visual interface for GitHub (recommended if you're new to this). Download from: [desktop.github.com](https://desktop.github.com) ### Step 4 — Clone your fork to your computer In GitHub Desktop: 1. Click **File → Clone repository** 2. Find your fork (`YOURUSERNAME/ps50-blog`) 3. Choose a folder on your computer 4. Click **Clone** You now have the whole site on your machine. Your post file will live in the `posts/` folder. --- ## Choose your stream {#streams} Every post belongs to one of three streams. Pick the one that fits how you want to write. ::: {.callout-note .callout-homework} ## Stream 1 — Homework **For:** Well-organised academic writing, published properly. **What it looks like:** A structured essay with a clear argument, in-text citations, a reference list, and some basic formatting (headings, callout boxes). Readable by anyone, not just academics. **Extra features used:** Callout boxes, citation integration, floating TOC, author bio. **A Homework post done well is indistinguishable from something you'd read on The Conversation.** ::: ::: {.callout-tip .callout-publication} ## Stream 2 — Publication **For:** Writing aimed at a real general audience. Think science journalism. **What it looks like:** A magazine-style article. Strong opening hook, no jargon (or jargon explained), pull quotes, images, a clear narrative arc. Citations exist but are unobtrusive. **Extra features used:** Pull quotes, hero image, tabset sections, TL;DR summary box, Hypothesis comments. **A Publication post could be submitted to a student science magazine or pitched to Psyche.** ::: ::: {.callout-important .callout-creator} ## Stream 3 — Signal **For:** Content-creator mode. Data, interactivity, video, a strong voice. **What it looks like:** An engaging piece built to be shared. Could include live R code readers can run in the browser, interactive charts, an embedded video of you explaining something, a bold visual identity. **Extra features used:** WebR interactive code, Observable JS charts, embedded video, Bluesky/share links. **A Signal post is built to travel. It's the kind of thing that gets screenshot and shared.** ::: --- ## Create your post file {#create} ### The file structure Your post needs to live inside a subfolder of `posts/`. The folder name becomes part of the URL. For example: ``` posts/ your-name-post-title/ index.qmd ← your post data.csv ← any data files (optional) images/ ← any images (optional) ``` Create a new folder inside `posts/` named with your surname and a short title slug, like: `posts/wright-deception-detection/` ### Copy the right template Three template files are included in the repo root. Copy the one matching your stream into your folder and rename it `index.qmd`. | Stream | Template file | |--------|--------------| | Homework | `_template-homework.qmd` | | Publication | `_template-publication.qmd` | | Signal | `_template-signal.qmd` | Open `index.qmd` in RStudio and start editing. --- ## Writing your post {#writing} Everything below explains the features available in each stream, with copy-paste code snippets. ### The YAML header (all streams) The very top of your `.qmd` file is the YAML header — this is metadata that controls how your post appears on the site. It must start and end with `---`. ```yaml --- title: "The title of your post" author: "Your Name" date: "2025-03-01" description: "One sentence that appears in the listing. Make it good." categories: [social psychology, memory, cognition] image: "images/cover.jpg" # optional but recommended stream: homework # homework | publication | signal --- ``` ::: {.callout-warning} ## Date format Use `YYYY-MM-DD` format exactly. Other formats will cause the site to break. ::: --- ### Stream 1 — Homework features {#homework} homework A Homework post is a well-written piece of academic work that happens to be published on the web. The features below are all straightforward and don't require any code. #### Callout boxes Use callout boxes to highlight key ideas, add definitions, flag caveats, or add your own opinion clearly separated from the main argument. ```markdown ::: {.callout-note} ## Key concept The **hindsight bias** is the tendency to believe, after an event has occurred, that one would have predicted or expected the outcome beforehand. ::: ::: {.callout-tip} ## My take I find this particularly relevant for eyewitness testimony research, where confidence in a memory often increases *after* feedback from investigators. ::: ::: {.callout-warning} ## Methodological caveat Most studies in this area rely on WEIRD samples (Western, Educated, Industrialised, Rich, Democratic). Generalisability is limited. ::: ::: {.callout-important} ## Core finding Loftus & Palmer (1974) demonstrated that the wording of a question can significantly alter participants' memories of an event. ::: ``` **Callout types:** `callout-note` (blue), `callout-tip` (green), `callout-warning` (orange), `callout-important` (red). Add `collapse="true"` to make a callout collapsible: ```markdown ::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"} ## Click to reveal the definition Content hidden until clicked. ::: ``` #### Citations Quarto handles citations properly if you have a `.bib` file. Add this to your YAML: ```yaml bibliography: references.bib ``` Then cite in text with `[@authorYear]`: ```markdown Working memory capacity is strongly predictive of fluid intelligence [@conway2003]. Multiple studies have failed to replicate the ego depletion effect [@hagger2016; @lurquin2016]. ``` At the end of your post, add: ```markdown ## References ::: {#refs} ::: ``` Quarto automatically formats the reference list in APA style. ::: {.callout-tip} ## Getting .bib files from Zotero In Zotero: select your sources → right-click → Export Items → Format: BibTeX → Save as `references.bib` in your post folder. ::: #### Floating table of contents The TOC is on by default for this site. Make sure your post uses `##` headings (not just bold text) so the TOC populates correctly. #### Author bio Paste this at the very bottom of your post and fill in your details: ```markdown ::: {.author-card} ![](images/you.jpg)
Your Name
Psychology student at Goldsmiths, University of London. Interested in [your research interests]. [Optional: link to ORCID or LinkedIn]
::: ``` --- ### Stream 2 — Publication features {#publication} publication A Publication post is written for a real general audience. Every feature here is about pulling the reader in and keeping them. #### TL;DR summary box Put this right after the YAML header, before you start writing. It tells busy readers the point immediately. ```markdown ::: {.tldr} - We're not as good at detecting lies as we think we are - Decades of research puts human lie detection accuracy around 54% — barely above chance - Training doesn't help much, and confidence makes things worse ::: ``` #### Pull quote Extract a key sentence from your post and display it large: ```markdown ::: {.pullquote} "The uncomfortable truth is that lie detectors — human or machine — perform only marginally better than a coin flip." ::: ``` #### Tabset sections Use tabs to organise dense content without making readers scroll through everything: ```markdown ::: {.panel-tabset} ## The research Main findings explained here. Keep jargon-free. ## What it means So-what paragraph. Connect to the reader's life. ## The limitations Honest appraisal of what the studies can't tell us. ::: ``` #### Hero image Add an image at the top with a caption that pulls double duty as a lede: ```markdown ![A surveillance camera above a busy street. Most of us walk past these confident we could spot a liar. The research suggests otherwise. *(Photo: [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com))*](images/cover.jpg) ``` #### Hypothesis comments Comments are enabled site-wide via [Hypothesis](https://web.hypothes.is). Readers can annotate your post. You don't need to do anything — it's on by default. Readers click the sidebar on the right to leave annotations. #### Share link (Bluesky) Paste this at the end of your post to give readers a one-click way to share it: ```markdown --- *If this post made you think, you can [share it on Bluesky](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=Interesting+read+from+%40ps50blog%3A+PASTE-YOUR-URL-HERE).* ``` --- ### Stream 3 — Signal features {#signal} signal A Signal post is built to travel. It has data, interactivity, or video — something that makes it more than words on a screen. #### Interactive R code with WebR WebR lets readers run and edit R code directly in their browser. No R installation needed. This is remarkable — you can show your data analysis and let readers reproduce it. First, add this to your YAML: ```yaml filters: - webr webr: packages: ['ggplot2', 'dplyr'] ``` Then use `{webr-r}` chunks instead of `{r}`: ````markdown ```{webr-r} # Readers can edit and run this code themselves scores <- c(54, 58, 61, 49, 72, 55, 67, 50, 63, 58) hist(scores, main = "Distribution of lie-detection accuracy scores", xlab = "% correct", col = "#3d5afe", border = "white") abline(v = mean(scores), col = "#d81b60", lwd = 2, lty = 2) ``` ```` Use `#| autorun: true` to run the code automatically when the page loads: ````markdown ```{webr-r} #| autorun: true #| read-only: true cat("Chance-level performance would be 50%.\n") cat("Meta-analytic mean across 206 studies:", 53.7, "%\n") ``` ```` ::: {.callout-note} ## Setup code (hidden from readers) Use `context: setup` for code that loads data but shouldn't clutter the post: ````markdown ```{webr-r} #| context: setup set.seed(42) n <- 120 study_data <- data.frame( condition = rep(c("control", "training", "feedback"), each = 40), accuracy = c( rnorm(40, 54, 8), rnorm(40, 56, 9), rnorm(40, 52, 7) ) ) ``` ```` Then later chunks can use `study_data` without showing the setup. ::: #### Observable JS charts For slick interactive charts without any R, use Observable JS. This runs entirely in the browser: ````markdown ```{ojs} // An interactive histogram readers can explore data = [54, 58, 61, 49, 72, 55, 67, 50, 63, 58, 71, 48, 59, 62, 55] Plot.plot({ marks: [ Plot.rectY(data, Plot.binX({y: "count"}, {x: d => d, fill: "#3d5afe"})), Plot.ruleY([0]) ], x: {label: "Accuracy (%)"}, y: {label: "Count"}, width: 640 }) ``` ```` #### Embed a video Record yourself explaining your post's key finding in 60–90 seconds. Upload to YouTube or Vimeo, then embed: ```markdown {{< video https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YOUR_VIDEO_ID >}} ``` Or for a local video file: ```markdown {{< video images/my-explainer.mp4 >}} ``` ::: {.callout-tip} ## Easy screen + face recording **Loom** (free for students) lets you record your screen and face simultaneously, generates a shareable link immediately, and the embed code works directly in Quarto. Go to [loom.com](https://loom.com). ::: #### ggplot2 chart (rendered at build time) If you'd rather not use WebR, you can include a regular R chart that gets rendered once when the site builds: ````markdown ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Human lie detection accuracy across 206 studies (Vrij, 2008)](contributing_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){fig-alt='Bar chart showing accuracy rates across studies clustering around 54%' width=672} ::: ::: ```` --- ## Preview your post locally Before submitting, check it actually renders. In RStudio's terminal: ```bash quarto preview ``` This opens the whole site in your browser. Navigate to your post and check that everything looks right — no broken images, code runs, citations appear. To render just your post: ```bash quarto render posts/your-name-post-title/index.qmd ``` --- ## Submit your post (pull request) {#submit} When you're happy with your post: ### Step 1 — Commit your changes In GitHub Desktop: 1. You'll see your new files listed on the left under "Changes" 2. Write a short summary at the bottom: `Add post: Your Post Title` 3. Click **Commit to main** ### Step 2 — Push to your fork Click **Push origin** (the button at the top of GitHub Desktop). This sends your changes to your fork on GitHub. ### Step 3 — Open a pull request 1. Go to your fork on GitHub: `https://github.com/YOURUSERNAME/ps50-blog` 2. You'll see a yellow banner: "This branch is 1 commit ahead of YOURNAME:main" 3. Click **Contribute → Open pull request** 4. Fill in the title: your post title 5. In the description box, add: - Your stream (Homework / Publication / Signal) - One sentence about your post - Any notes for the reviewer (e.g., "I used WebR — please check it renders") 6. Click **Create pull request** That's it. You'll get a notification when it's reviewed and merged. ::: {.callout-warning} ## Before you submit, check: - [ ] Your post is in `posts/your-name-post-title/index.qmd` - [ ] The YAML header has title, author, date, description, categories, and stream - [ ] `quarto preview` runs without errors - [ ] Images are in your post's `images/` subfolder (not somewhere else on your computer) - [ ] If you used WebR, test that the code chunks actually run - [ ] You've added a CC BY 4.0 licence line at the bottom (see templates) ::: --- ## Getting help **Quarto documentation:** [quarto.org/docs/websites](https://quarto.org/docs/websites) **WebR documentation:** [docs.r-wasm.org/webr](https://docs.r-wasm.org/webr/latest/) **GitHub help:** [docs.github.com](https://docs.github.com) If something is broken and you can't fix it, open an Issue on the repository rather than emailing — other students might have the same problem and the answer will be visible to everyone.
